threats.title: ETH Fallback in _transferOutAndCallV5 Sends Funds to Aggregator Target Instead of User Recipient
threats.hypothesis_description: When the aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails in the ETH path of _transferOutAndCallV5, the fallback sends ETH to `aggregationPayload.target` (the aggregator contract) instead of `aggregationPayload.recipient` (the intended user). This is a regression from V4's transferOutAndCall which correctly falls back to the recipient (`to`). Any failed aggregator swap results in user ETH being sent to an unintended contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect visible by comparing V4 and V5 fallback logic. The V4 `transferOutAndCall` fallback sends to `to` (user), while V5 sends to `target` (aggregator). Failed swaps are a normal operational occurrence (slippage, liquidity issues), making this a high-frequency path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Wrong recipient in fallback: sends to aggregator target instead of user recipient

preconditions: Vault calls transferOutAndCallV5 or batchTransferOutAndCallV5 with ETH
Aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails (slippage, liquidity, revert)
aggregationPayload.target != aggregationPayload.recipient

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User's ETH is sent to aggregator contract on swap failure instead of being returned to user
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that target == recipient, no recipient fallback

analysis_questions: How frequently do aggregator swapOutV5 calls fail in production?
Do all whitelisted aggregators have rescue/withdrawal mechanisms?
Can an aggregator intentionally fail to keep ETH?
validation_requirements: Deploy test with failing aggregator and verify ETH goes to target not recipient
Compare V4 and V5 fallback behavior side by side
Quantify ETH at risk based on historical failed swap frequency

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault sends ETH via transferOutAndCallV5. Aggregator swap fails due to slippage. ETH is sent to aggregator contract instead of user. If aggregator has no withdrawal mechanism or owner is unresponsive, user funds are permanently lost.
prerequisites: Active vault performing ETH swaps via aggregator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full ETH amount of any failed aggregator swap is misdirected
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user receiving ETH via cross-chain swap through an aggregator
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on aggregator having rescueFunds; no router-side recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying fallback recipient in V5 ETH path vs V4

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing TransferOutAndCallV5 event with on-chain ETH flow

threats.title: ERC20 Tokens Irrecoverably Lost When Aggregator swapOutV5 Fails in _transferOutAndCallV5
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 path, tokens are transferred to the aggregator via `transfer()`, then `swapOutV5` is called. If swapOutV5 fails, the return value `_dexAggSuccess` is completely ignored and no fallback exists. Tokens remain stranded at the aggregator with no router-side recovery mechanism. The transaction succeeds and emits events as if the operation completed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code comment explicitly acknowledges this design ('if the aggregator fails, it should not revert the whole transaction') but provides no recovery path. Unlike the ETH path which has a fallback (albeit to the wrong address), the ERC20 path has zero fallback logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unchecked return value after token transfer; no recovery on failure

preconditions: Vault calls _transferOutAndCallV5 with ERC20 fromAsset
ERC20 transfer to aggregator succeeds
Aggregator swapOutV5 fails for any reason

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Tokens transferred to aggregator, then swap called, failure silently ignored
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _dexAggSuccess is declared but never used in any conditional logic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback transfer to recipient, no revert on failure, no recovery mechanism

analysis_questions: What percentage of aggregator swaps fail in production?
Do all whitelisted aggregators implement rescueFunds for ERC20?
Could a malicious aggregator be whitelisted and intentionally fail swaps to steal tokens?
validation_requirements: Test with THORChain_Failing_Aggregator to confirm tokens are stranded
Verify there's no secondary mechanism to recover tokens
Check if Bifrost detects and retries failed aggregator swaps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault sends ERC20 via _transferOutAndCallV5. Aggregator's swapOutV5 reverts. Tokens are already at aggregator. Transaction succeeds, event emitted, but user never receives anything. Tokens locked at aggregator.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full ERC20 amount of any failed aggregator swap is stranded
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user receiving ERC20 via cross-chain aggregated swap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only if aggregator has rescueFunds accessible to owner

threats.test_coverage_gap: Test coverage for ERC20 aggregator failure path

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing event emissions with actual token receipts

threats.title: msg.value Reused in batchTransferOutAndCallV5 Loop Causing Double-Spend or ETH Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: In batchTransferOutAndCallV5, each iteration calls _transferOutAndCallV5 which uses `msg.value` for the ETH value sent to the aggregator. If the batch contains multiple entries with fromAsset == address(0), each call sends the full msg.value. The first call sends all ETH; subsequent calls attempt to send msg.value again, potentially draining any ETH held by the router or failing on fallback.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop iterates over all payloads, and each ETH-path call sends msg.value (the total ETH for the entire batch), not each entry's individual fromAmount. This is a classic msg.value-in-loop vulnerability.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.value used inside loop iteration; full value sent on every ETH-path call

preconditions: Vault calls batchTransferOutAndCallV5 with multiple ETH-based entries
msg.value > 0
At least two entries have fromAsset == address(0)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: THORChain_Router.sol batchTransferOutAndCallV5 loop and _transferOutAndCallV5 ETH branch
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: msg.value reused per iteration; each call{value: msg.value} sends full amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: First aggregator call receives all ETH; subsequent calls may drain router balance or fail
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-entry ETH tracking; no validation that sum of fromAmounts == msg.value

analysis_questions: Do THORChain nodes ever batch multiple ETH aggregation calls?
What happens to the router if subsequent iterations drain its balance?
Could an attacker influence batch composition?
validation_requirements: Test batch with two ETH aggregation calls
Verify msg.value is sent on each iteration
Check if router ETH balance is drained

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault submits batch with 2 ETH aggregation calls, each for 5 ETH, msg.value = 10 ETH. First call sends 10 ETH to aggregator. Second call sends 10 ETH again but router only has residual, causing fallback cascade.
prerequisites: Batch with multiple ETH-based aggregation entries

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: First aggregator receives inflated ETH amount; subsequent entries receive wrong amounts or fail
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users in the same batch as the ETH swap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Excess ETH may be stuck at aggregator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for batch with multiple ETH aggregation entries

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by tracing ETH flows in batch transactions

threats.title: Anyone Can Drain ERC20 Tokens From THORChain_Aggregator via swapOutV5
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Aggregator.swapOutV5 is public and permissionless. In the ERC20 path (fromAsset != address(0)), it approves the swapRouter to spend `fromAmount` of `fromAsset` tokens held by the aggregator, then calls swapRouter via low-level call (making msg.sender = aggregator from swapRouter's perspective). An attacker can call swapOutV5 with fromAsset set to any ERC20 the aggregator holds and fromAmount set to the aggregator's balance, draining the tokens through the DEX swap to a recipient they control.
threats.investigation_rationale: ERC20 tokens accumulate in the aggregator from failed _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 swaps (TECH-H002). The swapOutV5 function has no access control and approves+spends from its own balance. This creates a compounding vulnerability where failed swaps deposit tokens and anyone can sweep them.

proof_of_signal.lines: swapOutV5 ERC20 branch: `safeApprove(fromAsset, address(swapRouter), fromAmount)` then `address(swapRouter).call(abi.encodeWithSignature("swapExactTokensForETH(...)", fromAmount, ...))`
proof_of_signal.pattern: Permissionless function approves and spends aggregator's own token balance via DEX

preconditions: THORChain_Aggregator holds ERC20 tokens (from failed swaps, direct transfers, etc.)
DEX liquidity exists for the token pair
Attacker calls swapOutV5 with fromAsset = token address and fromAmount = aggregator's balance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No access control; approves swapRouter for fromAmount then calls swap spending aggregator's tokens
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can trigger swaps using aggregator's existing token holdings
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No caller validation, no check that tokens were received in this call

analysis_questions: How often do tokens accumulate in the aggregator from failed swaps?
Is there a monitoring system to detect and rescue stranded tokens?
Could an attacker atomically send tokens + call swapOutV5 for profit?
validation_requirements: Send ERC20 to aggregator, then call swapOutV5 from a different account
Verify tokens are swapped and ETH sent to attacker-controlled recipient
Test with tokens from a simulated failed _transferOutAndCallV5

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Router's _transferOutAndCallV5 sends ERC20 to aggregator, swap fails, tokens stranded. Attacker calls swapOutV5(tokenAddress, aggregatorBalance, WETH, attackerAddress, 0, '', '') to drain all tokens through DEX to their address.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of all ERC20 tokens held by the aggregator
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose tokens were stranded from failed aggregator swaps
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None once tokens are drained via DEX swap

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for unauthorized swapOutV5 calls with aggregator-held tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as swapOutV5 call from non-router address

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Deposits Entire Contract ETH Balance Including Pre-existing Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn, after the DEX swap, `_safeAmount = address(this).balance` captures the entire ETH balance of the aggregator contract, not just the output of the current swap. Any pre-existing ETH (from direct sends, failed operations, or other sources) is attributed to the current caller's deposit. An attacker can steal any ETH sitting in the aggregator by calling swapIn with minimal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The aggregator has a `receive() external payable` function allowing anyone to send ETH to it. Failed ETH fallbacks from router operations could also leave ETH in the aggregator. The use of address(this).balance instead of tracking the delta means all accumulated ETH is swept into the attacker's deposit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses total contract balance instead of swap output delta

preconditions: Aggregator contract holds pre-existing ETH/AVAX
Attacker has some ERC20 tokens to initiate a swap
DEX has liquidity for the token->WETH pair

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: address(this).balance includes pre-existing ETH, not just swap output
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All ETH in aggregator is deposited under caller's memo
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre/post balance delta tracking for ETH

analysis_questions: How does ETH accumulate in the aggregator in practice?
Is this exploitable atomically (send ETH + call swapIn in same tx)?
Does the AvaxAggregator have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to aggregator, then call swapIn with dust tokens
Verify the deposit includes the pre-existing ETH
Confirm AvaxAggregator has identical pattern

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Aggregator accumulates 1 ETH from failed operations. Attacker calls swapIn with $1 worth of tokens, receives ~$1 ETH from swap. Deposit sends entire aggregator balance (~1.001 ETH) to THORChain under attacker's memo, netting ~1 ETH profit.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of all pre-existing ETH/AVAX in the aggregator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for swapIn with pre-existing aggregator balance

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal token balance for DEX swap
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing swap output with deposit amount

threats.title: batchTransferOutV5 ETH Accounting Mismatch: Uses Struct Amount Instead of msg.value
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferOutV5, the ETH path sends `transferOutPayload.amount` (from the struct), not msg.value. In batchTransferOutV5, if the sum of ETH amounts in the batch doesn't equal msg.value, there's an accounting mismatch. Excess msg.value is permanently locked in the router. If msg.value is less than the sum but the router has residual ETH from other operations, that ETH is consumed. Compare with V4 transferOut which uses `safeAmount = msg.value`.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a behavioral difference between V4 (uses msg.value) and V5 (uses struct amount). The router contract has no ETH withdrawal mechanism, so any excess ETH sent with batch calls is permanently locked. The inconsistency suggests a design defect.

proof_of_signal.lines: _transferOutV5 ETH path: `transferOutPayload.to.send(transferOutPayload.amount)` vs transferOut V4: `safeAmount = msg.value`
proof_of_signal.pattern: V5 batch uses struct amount not msg.value; no sum validation

preconditions: Vault calls batchTransferOutV5 with ETH entries
msg.value != sum of ETH amounts in batch

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Uses transferOutPayload.amount instead of msg.value for ETH send
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Excess msg.value permanently locked; deficit causes failure or drains residual ETH
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that msg.value == sum of ETH amounts in batch

analysis_questions: Do Bifrost nodes always send correct msg.value matching struct amounts?
Is there any ETH recovery mechanism for the router?
How much ETH could accumulate from mismatches over time?
validation_requirements: Test batchTransferOutV5 with msg.value > sum of ETH amounts
Verify excess ETH is locked in router
Test single transferOutV5 with msg.value != payload.amount

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Over time, Bifrost nodes send slightly mismatched msg.value with batch transfers. Excess ETH accumulates in the router with no recovery mechanism, representing permanent protocol losses.
prerequisites: Vault sends batch with msg.value != sum of ETH entries

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent ETH lock in router from mismatched msg.value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No withdrawal mechanism exists for locked ETH

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for msg.value vs struct amount mismatch in batch V5

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring router ETH balance over time

threats.title: Router _routerDeposit safeApprove Incompatible with USDT Approve Race Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: The _routerDeposit function calls safeApprove(asset, router, amount) without first resetting the approval to 0. USDT's approve() function requires the current allowance to be 0 before setting a new non-zero value. If any stale allowance exists (from a partially failed previous migration or deliberate griefing), USDT migration via _routerDeposit will permanently revert, blocking vault churn for USDT.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDT is explicitly mentioned in the project documentation as a supported token. The router is expected to handle 'weird' ERC20 behaviors. _routerDeposit is critical for vault churn and router migration. A blocked migration could strand USDT funds indefinitely.
deployment_assumptions: USDT or similar approve-race-protected tokens are used with the router
threats.config_evidence: USDT.sol in scope; project documentation explicitly states support for USDT-like tokens including 'non_standard_return' and blocklist behaviors

proof_of_signal.lines: _routerDeposit: `safeApprove(_asset, _router, _amount)` - single approve call without reset to 0
proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing approve(0) before approve(amount) for USDT compatibility

preconditions: Token is USDT or similar with approve race protection
Any stale allowance exists from router to new router address
Vault calls transferAllowance or returnVaultAssets with router != address(this)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: safeApprove does approve(router, amount) without first doing approve(router, 0)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: USDT approve reverts if current allowance != 0 and new value != 0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No approve reset to 0; no special handling for USDT-like tokens

analysis_questions: Can stale allowances accumulate from partial failures?
Could an attacker deliberately create a stale allowance to block migration?
Does AvaxRouter have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Test _routerDeposit with USDT mock that enforces approve(0) requirement
Test with pre-existing non-zero allowance to new router
Verify AvaxRouter _routerDeposit has same pattern

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During vault churn, vault calls returnVaultAssets to migrate USDT to new router. If there's any stale approval from a previous attempt, the USDT approve call reverts, blocking migration. All USDT funds are stranded in the old router.
prerequisites: Stale allowance exists or is created

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to migrate USDT during vault churn
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would require deploying a new migration path

threats.title: AvaxAggregator swapIn safeApprove Reverts on Non-Zero to Non-Zero Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxAggregator uses OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20.safeApprove which reverts if the current allowance is non-zero and the new value is also non-zero. If a previous swapIn left a stale approval (e.g., due to fee-on-transfer where _safeAmount < amount, leaving leftover approval), subsequent swapIn calls for the same token will revert permanently.
threats.investigation_rationale: The swapIn function calls `IERC20(token).safeApprove(address(swapRouter), amount)` using the original `amount` not the `safeAmount` (actual received). For fee-on-transfer tokens, `safeAmount < amount`, so the DEX swap uses `safeAmount` but the full `amount` was approved. The leftover approval (amount - safeAmount) persists, causing the next safeApprove to revert.
deployment_assumptions: Fee-on-transfer tokens are used with AvaxAggregator
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states protocol expects to handle fee-on-transfer tokens

proof_of_signal.lines: swapIn: `IERC20(token).safeApprove(address(swapRouter), amount)` - uses amount not safeAmount
proof_of_signal.pattern: OZ safeApprove reverts on non-zero to non-zero; stale approval from fee-on-transfer tokens

preconditions: Fee-on-transfer token is used
Previous swapIn left stale approval due to amount > safeAmount mismatch
Subsequent swapIn call for same token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: safeApprove(amount) where amount != safeAmount for fee-on-transfer tokens
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Leftover approval makes future safeApprove calls revert

analysis_questions: Which fee-on-transfer tokens are supported on Avalanche?
Does THORChain_Aggregator have the same issue? (It does approve(amount) not safeApprove)
Can this be triggered by normal operations?
validation_requirements: Test swapIn with fee-on-transfer token mock
Verify stale approval persists after first swap
Confirm second swapIn reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls swapIn with a 1% fee-on-transfer token. swapRouter consumes safeAmount but amount was approved. 1% leftover approval persists. Next user calling swapIn for same token gets permanent revert, DoS-ing that token's swap path.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of swapIn for specific fee-on-transfer tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users trying to swap affected tokens through AvaxAggregator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No fee-on-transfer token tests for AvaxAggregator

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when swapIn transactions start reverting

threats.title: AvaxRouter deposit() Is Public, Bypassing Expiry Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxRouter's deposit() function is public, unlike THORChain_Router where _deposit is private (only accessible through depositWithExpiry). Users or contracts calling deposit() directly on AvaxRouter bypass the expiration check, allowing stale transactions to execute at unfavorable times. THORChain_Router V5 deliberately made _deposit private to enforce expiry.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an inconsistency between the two router implementations. The THORChain_Router was updated to make _deposit private specifically to enforce the expiry requirement per the stated invariant. AvaxRouter was not updated to match, creating a security gap.
threats.config_evidence: deploy.ts deploys AvaxRouter; hardhat.config.ts targets Avalanche C-Chain (chainId 43114)

proof_of_signal.lines: deposit() is `public payable nonReentrant` vs THORChain_Router._deposit is `private nonReentrant`
proof_of_signal.pattern: Public deposit function bypasses expiration enforcement

preconditions: User or contract calls deposit() directly on AvaxRouter
Transaction is delayed past intended expiration

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Public visibility allows bypassing depositWithExpiry's timestamp check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Stale deposits can execute at unfavorable rates
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No expiration parameter on deposit(); should be private like THORChain_Router

analysis_questions: Do any contracts or integrations call deposit() directly?
What's the maximum delay for Avalanche C-Chain transactions?
Is this intentional for backward compatibility?
validation_requirements: Confirm deposit() is callable without expiry
Compare with THORChain_Router to confirm intentional divergence or oversight

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User's deposit transaction sits in mempool during high congestion. Market conditions change significantly. Transaction executes hours later via deposit() without expiry check, resulting in unfavorable swap rate.
prerequisites: Direct call to deposit() instead of depositWithExpiry

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users may execute deposits at unfavorable rates

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing deposit vs depositWithExpiry expiry enforcement on AvaxRouter

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring deposit() calls without expiry

threats.title: THORChain_Aggregator swapIn Over-Approves SwapRouter for Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn, after receiving tokens via safeTransferFrom (which correctly accounts for fees via balance delta returning _safeAmount), the approve call uses the original `amount` parameter rather than `_safeAmount`. This creates a stale leftover approval of (amount - _safeAmount) on the swapRouter, which could be exploited by the swapRouter or by anyone who can manipulate the swapRouter to spend the excess approval.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly handles fee-on-transfer tokens in safeTransferFrom but then ignores the safe amount for approval. While the swap itself uses _safeAmount, the over-approval persists and could be consumed by future interactions with the same swapRouter address.
deployment_assumptions: Fee-on-transfer tokens are used with the aggregator
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states protocol expects to handle fee-on-transfer tokens

proof_of_signal.pattern: Approval amount exceeds actual token balance received

preconditions: Fee-on-transfer token is used
safeAmount < amount due to transfer fee

analysis_questions: Can the stale approval be exploited by the swapRouter or a third party?
Does this accumulate over multiple swapIn calls?
Is the swapRouter trusted enough that this doesn't matter?
validation_requirements: Test swapIn with fee-on-transfer token
Verify stale approval persists after swap
Assess if swapRouter can exploit the excess approval

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Repeated swapIn calls with fee-on-transfer tokens accumulate stale approvals on swapRouter. If swapRouter is compromised or has a vulnerability, the accumulated stale approvals could be used to drain tokens from the aggregator.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stale approval could be exploited if swapRouter is compromised
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users with tokens in the aggregator pipeline

threats.test_coverage_gap: No fee-on-transfer token tests for swapIn approval amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking allowance after swapIn

threats.title: ETH_RUNE transferTo Uses tx.origin Enabling Token Theft via Intermediary Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: ETH_RUNE's transferTo function uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender, allowing any contract in the call chain to transfer tokens from the original transaction signer without their explicit consent or approval. A malicious contract that a user interacts with for any reason can silently drain their entire ETH.RUNE balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a well-documented anti-pattern (SWC-115). The code comment acknowledges the risk but dismisses it as equivalent to infinite approvals. However, unlike infinite approvals, this requires zero user consent - any interaction with any malicious contract suffices. The documentation says ETH.RUNE is deprecated and out of scope, but the code is in the auditable contracts list.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tx.origin used as source of funds instead of msg.sender

preconditions: User holds ETH.RUNE tokens
User interacts with any contract (even unrelated to RUNE)
That contract calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attackerAddress, amount)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any intermediary contract can drain user's RUNE without approval
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use msg.sender or require explicit approval

analysis_questions: How many users still hold ETH.RUNE?
Is ETH.RUNE used in any active protocol flows?
Has this been exploited historically?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious contract that calls transferTo during normal interaction
Confirm victim's RUNE is transferred without their knowledge

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a DeFi contract. User calls any function on it. The contract calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attackerAddress, victimBalance), draining all the user's ETH.RUNE tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of victim's ETH.RUNE balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any ETH.RUNE holder who interacts with malicious contracts

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for transferTo exploitation via intermediary contract

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as transferTo call from non-user address

threats.title: Permissionless Event Spoofing via Dust Deposits Tricks Bifrost Observation
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address can call depositWithExpiry to emit Deposit events with arbitrary vault addresses and memos for minimal cost. For ETH deposits, sending 1 wei emits a legitimate Deposit event. For ERC20 deposits, any token with approval emits the event. If Bifrost's log parser doesn't strictly validate minimum thresholds, vault whitelist, and memo semantics, crafted events could trigger unintended cross-chain actions or create noise disrupting observation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly flags log spoofing as a risk area. The router has no on-chain access control for deposits. The author-flagged risk mentions 'Malicious smart contracts tricking the Bifrost smartcontract_log_parser into registering a fake DepositEvent'. The defense relies entirely on Bifrost off-chain validation.

proof_of_signal.lines: depositWithExpiry/deposit: `emit Deposit(vault, asset, safeAmount, memo)` - no access control on who can emit
proof_of_signal.pattern: Permissionless event emission with arbitrary parameters

preconditions: Attacker has minimal ETH or approved ERC20 tokens
Bifrost's log parser processes events from any sender

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No sender validation for deposit event emission
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can emit Deposit events with crafted memos to any vault address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum deposit amount, no sender whitelist on-chain

analysis_questions: Does Bifrost validate minimum deposit amounts per asset?
Does Bifrost verify the depositor address or only the event source contract?
Can crafted memos trigger unintended THORChain L1 actions?
validation_requirements: Emit dust Deposit events with various memos and verify Bifrost handling
Check if max_contract_tx_logs limit prevents log flooding attacks
Verify minimum threshold enforcement in Bifrost

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a contract that calls depositWithExpiry with 1 wei ETH to a vault address with crafted swap memos. If Bifrost processes these without adequate filtering, it could trigger unintended cross-chain swaps or state transitions on THORChain L1.
prerequisites: Bifrost processes events below dust threshold

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential for cross-chain state confusion or unintended swap processing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on THORChain L1 validation layers

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for dust deposit event processing by Bifrost

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can flood events across multiple blocks
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring deposits below dust threshold
